1: Chapter 22 continues the regulations for the priestly office, shifting from personal qualifications (ch. 21) to ritual qualifications for eating sacred food and presenting acceptable sacrifices. The chapter operates at two levels: (1) the priestly household's access to the holy portions (qodashim), and (2) the physical integrity of animals brought as offerings. Both sections share the same principle: holiness demands wholeness, and what is given to YHWH must be the best, not the defective or disqualified.
2: yinnazru (Niphal imperfect of nazar, 'to separate, consecrate, dedicate'): 'Aaron and his sons shall keep away from the holy things of the people of Israel.' The root nzr gives us both nazir (Nazirite, one who separates himself by vow) and nezer (the high priest's diadem/crown). The same root covers both voluntary consecration and required separation. Here the Niphal (reflexive passive) means the priests must separate themselves — in context, this means while ritually impure they must abstain from the holy offerings. Holiness and impurity are mutually exclusive poles; the priest cannot stand in both simultaneously.
3: nitmetah (Niphal of tame): ritual defilement cuts a priest off from the holy food. The text says such a priest will be 'cut off' (nikhretah) from YHWH's presence — the same karet language used for the gravest covenant violations. This reflects the seriousness of holiness-boundary violations even within the priestly system: the remedy is purification and waiting, but there is no shortcut. The priest who eats holy food while impure is not merely ritually irregular — he has treated the holy as common (chillel).
4: The list of disqualifying impurities — tzara'at (skin disease), genital discharge (zob), contact with a corpse-contaminated person, semen emission — mirrors the purity laws of chapters 13-15. The common thread is that all these conditions involve either death (corpse, skin disease), reproductive disruption, or bodily emission — all associated with mortality and the body's boundaries. Holiness is associated with life, wholeness, and integrity; these conditions mark the opposite. The solution is time, washing, and waiting for evening — the body's natural restoration cycle.
8: nevela (carcass of an animal that died naturally) and trefah (an animal torn by wild beasts) — these two categories are prohibited food for priests, though they could be given to the resident alien or sold to a foreigner (Deut 14:21; contrast with Lev 22:8 prohibiting the priest from eating them). The issue is not the food category in the abstract but the priest's heightened holiness standard. Trefah later becomes the broad term in rabbinic halakha for any non-kosher meat; here it has its original specific meaning of an animal mauled by predators. The priest must not eat what was not killed in the prescribed sacrificial manner.
9: chet (sin/penalty) and metu bo (they will die because of it): the phrase 'they shall therefore keep my charge' (shamru et mishmarti) employs the standard custodial language of priestly responsibility. The doubling of the root in 'keep my charge' (mishmeret = 'what is to be kept/guarded') emphasizes the active, ongoing nature of priestly duty. Death as the penalty for mishandling holy things appears also in Numbers 4:15-20 (Kohathites), reinforcing that the Levitical system treated holiness violations as capital matters.
10: The category of those permitted to eat the terumah (priestly portion) is carefully defined: zar (outsider, non-priest), toshav (resident/tenant of the priest), and sakhir (hired laborer) are excluded. The contrast with permitted persons (v.11-13) shows the rule is about covenant status and household belonging, not ethnicity: a slave purchased by a priest or one born into his household — bound to the priestly household — may eat. The priestly household is a closed economy of holiness; membership matters more than origin.
14: be-shegagah (inadvertently, by mistake) — if a layman eats the holy food by accident, he must restore it at full value (chamishito yosif alav, 'he shall add a fifth to it'). The 20% surcharge appears also in guilt offering law (Lev 5:16) and restitution law (Lev 6:5). The principle: even unintentional violations of the holy have real consequences requiring real remedy. Grace does not mean consequences are erased; it means a path of restoration is provided. The fifth part (chamishit) signals that trespass against the holy incurs more than simple restitution — there is a premium for having encroached.
18: tamim (perfect, without blemish, whole): the term used throughout the offering laws for the required quality of sacrificial animals. Its root tmm means 'to be complete, perfect, whole' — the same root gives tom (integrity, wholeness, blamelessness) used of Job's character (Job 1:1) and the psalmist's walk (Ps 15:2). Applied to animals, tamim means without physical defect. Hebrews 9:14 applies the concept directly to Christ: he offered himself 'without blemish' (amōmon, the LXX translation of tamim) to God. The animal regulation of Lev 22:18-25 is the type that explains why Christ's offering could be effective where animal offerings could not.
20: lo leratzon yihyeh lachem (it will not be acceptable/pleasing for you): the passive sense of ratzon (pleasure, acceptance, favor) as the criterion for a valid offering. Ratzon comes from the root meaning 'to be pleased with, to accept favorably.' The acceptance of an offering by YHWH is not automatic — it depends on the integrity of what is offered and the integrity of the offerer. Isaiah 1:11-15 shows YHWH rejecting Israel's abundant offerings because the offerers' hands were bloodied — acceptability is not merely a ritual category but a relational one.
21: shelem (peace/fellowship offering): the term is from the root shalom (peace, wholeness, completeness). The peace offering is the only sacrifice where the offerer, the priests, and YHWH all receive portions — it is inherently communal. The three subtypes (todah/thanksgiving, neder/vow, nedavah/freewill) appear in ch. 7. The tamim requirement applies here too: lo yihyeh bo mum (there shall be no defect in it). The completeness of the peace offering's animal mirrors the shalom it embodies — communion with a holy God requires wholeness in what is brought.
22: The catalog of disqualifying blemishes: blind (iveret), injured (shavurah), maimed (charutz), running sore (yabbelet), scab (garav), scurvy (yallefet). The specificity of the list is important: it is not a general principle but an enumeration, preventing priestly discretion in accepting substandard animals. The purity of the offering system depends on clear, public, verifiable standards. The same principle appears in mal. 1:8 where Malachi condemns the priests of his era for accepting blemished animals — the law was meant to prevent precisely this corruption.
24: Prohibition on offering animals with crushed, torn, or cut testicles. The emasculation of animals was common in surrounding cultures for various purposes. The prohibition here may relate to the wholeness-requirement: the animal must be complete, not altered. In the Holiness Code's logic, cutting or crushing represents a deviation from created wholeness. Note that Deut 23:1 extends similar logic to human membership in the assembly: the eunuch is excluded from the assembly (though Isa 56:3-5 reverses this exclusion for eunuchs who keep the Sabbath and hold fast the covenant).
25: min ben nechar (from the hand of a foreigner): even sacrifices brought by resident aliens or non-Israelites must meet the tamim standard. This confirms that the blemish laws are not merely about Israelite social purity but about what is intrinsically acceptable to YHWH. The offering standard transcends the identity of the offerer. This aligns with the trajectory toward universal worship: Isaiah 56:7 envisions foreigners whose burnt offerings and sacrifices are accepted on YHWH's altar.
27: A newborn animal must remain seven days with its mother before it is eligible for offering. The seven-day waiting period mirrors the circumcision-timing (Lev 12:3) and possibly the completion-of-a-week principle that runs through the creation narrative. The prohibition on killing the mother and her young on the same day (v.28) has parallels in Deut 22:6-7 (the bird's nest law) — a concern for the natural order and against gratuitous destruction of reproductive capacity, reflecting the creation stewardship mandate.
28: oto ve-et beno lo tishchatu beyom echad (it and its young you shall not slaughter on the same day): the prohibition on killing parent and offspring the same day. This is one of several Levitical laws that recognize animal family bonds — not by anthropomorphizing animals but by maintaining a certain reverence for the natural order that YHWH created. Maimonides (Guide 3:48) explained it as prevention of cruelty; modern readers see it as ecological wisdom. The underlying logic is consistent with the Holiness Code's concern that Israel not act with callous disregard for creation.
29: toda (thanksgiving offering): a subtype of the peace offering in which the motivation is gratitude for a specific act of divine deliverance. The same-day eating rule (v.30) applies: leratzonkhem tizbachu — the urgency of consuming it reflects the immediacy and specificity of the thanksgiving. Psalm 107 is the great thanksgiving liturgy envisioning four types of delivered people bringing their toda; Ps 50:14 calls the toda the true sacrifice YHWH desires. The toda is the offering of relationship and response, not transaction.
30: mimacharat lo yinotir (you shall not leave any of it until morning): the time-constraint on the todah offering creates urgency that distinguishes it from the two-day peace offering. The perishability-limit on holy food prevents its commercialization: you cannot stockpile thanksgiving. The pattern also appears in the manna (Exod 16:19-20) — YHWH's provision is meant to be received and consumed in trust, not hoarded. Each day's grace is sufficient for that day.
31: ushemar'tem mitzvotai va-asitem otam (You shall keep my commandments and do them): the transition to the Holiness Code's ethical summary. The dual verb — keep (shamar) and do (asah) — is the standard covenant obedience formula: internalize and then act. Ani YHWH (I am YHWH) grounds the command in divine identity, not merely divine authority.
32: velo techallelu et shem qodshi veniqdashti betoch benei Yisrael: 'You shall not profane my holy name, but I will be hallowed among the people of Israel.' Chillul hashem (profaning the Name) and kiddush hashem (sanctifying/hallowing the Name) are the two poles of this verse — the most concentrated statement in the Torah of the concept that YHWH's reputation among the nations is at stake in Israel's behavior. The Niphal of qadash — 'I will be hallowed/sanctified' — is passive: YHWH's holiness is revealed through Israel's obedience. Ezekiel extensively develops this theme (Ezek 36:20-23): Israel's exile profaned YHWH's name among the nations; the restoration will vindicate it.
33: ani YHWH mekadishchem hamotzi etchem me-eretz Mitzrayim: 'I am YHWH who sanctifies you, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.' The closing formula links sanctification to the Exodus: YHWH's holiness-making activity in Israel is inseparable from his historical redemption of Israel. Israel was not first made holy and then redeemed — redemption came first (Exod 12-14), then the covenant and its holiness demands (Exod 19-Lev). The logic is grace → law, not law → grace.
5: o ish asher yiga bechol-sheretz asher yitma-lo: 'or who touches any swarming thing (sheretz) by which he becomes unclean.' The term sheretz links priestly purity law back to the creature taxonomy of ch11 — swarming creatures are the paradigmatic source of contact impurity. The priest who touches sheretz, a corpse, or a person with bodily discharge (vv4-5) is rendered tameh and must not eat of the holy offerings until purified. The list enumerates ascending degrees of severity, culminating in the prohibition on eating while in an impure state.
6: tameh yihyeh ad-ha'erev: 'he shall be unclean until evening.' This formula — tameh ad-ha'erev — recurs throughout Leviticus and Numbers as the standard duration of minor impurity; evening is the boundary of the impurity period. Unlike major impurities (corpse-contamination, scale disease, discharge) requiring seven-day waiting periods, these contact-based impurities resolve within a single day. velo yokhal min-haqodashim ki im-rachatz besaro bamayim: 'he shall not eat of the holy things unless he bathes his body in water' — immersion (rachatz basaro bamayim) is required before eating can resume; the passing of time alone is insufficient.
7: uva hashemesh vetaher: 'When the sun has set he shall be clean (tahor).' The formula completes the minor-impurity cycle: immersion + sunset = full purity restoration. Taher (to be clean) is the opposite of tameh; its use here describes the moment purity is fully restored, not merely reduced. ve'achar yokhal min-haqodashim: 'and afterward he may eat of the holy things' — access to priestly food resumes immediately at nightfall. The phrase ki lachmo hu ('for it is his food') signals that these regulations protect the priest's legitimate livelihood; the system of purity barriers guards the point of contact between the holy and the human, but the purpose is access, not exclusion.
11: vekohen ki-yiqneh nefesh qinyan kaspo: 'But if a priest buys a person as an acquisition (qinyan) with his money.' Qinyan (possession/acquisition) from the root q-n-h (to acquire) describes the permanent household membership that slave status creates — distinct from a hired worker (sachir) who is temporary. hu yokhal bo: 'that one may eat of it' — the slave is incorporated into the priestly household and shares in priestly food rights. The rule assumes that the priest's table is constituted by covenant household membership; the same principle applies to those born into the household (yelid beito).
12: uvat-kohen ki tihyeh le'ish zar: 'But if a priest's daughter is married to a layman (ish zar, lit. a foreign/outside man).' Zar (outsider/stranger — the same root as zarim used for non-Aaronides who must not approach the altar) marks the husband as outside the priestly circle. hi biterumot haqodashim lo tokhal: 'she may not eat of the contribution of the holy things.' Marriage to a non-priest transfers the woman out of the priestly household; she loses access to priestly food regardless of her birth. Terumah (contribution) is the priest's portion of agricultural produce — the archetypal priestly food that defines insider/outsider status.
13: uvat-kohen ki tihyeh almanah o gerushah: 'But if a priest's daughter is a widow (almanah) or divorced (gerushah).' uzara ein lah: 'and she has no offspring (zera, lit. seed).' Childlessness is the critical variable: a childless widow or divorcée has no tie binding her to her former husband's household and may return to her father's house. uvesuvah el-bet aviha kine'ureiha: 'and returns to her father's house as in her youth (kine'ureiha).' Once restored to the priestly household, she regains access to priestly food. The logic: food rights follow household membership. If she had children from the non-priestly marriage, those children are not of the priestly line — the law protects the restored daughter.
15: velo yechalelu et-qodshei benei-yisrael: 'The priests shall not profane (yechalelu, from chalal, to defile/pierce/make common) the holy offerings of the Israelites.' The verb chalal has the root meaning of piercing a boundary — bringing the holy into contact with the common in a way that dissolves its distinctiveness. asher yarimu laYHWH: 'that they lift up (rum) to YHWH' — the technical term terumah derives from this root, denoting something 'lifted off' the larger quantity and dedicated to YHWH.
16: vehisiu otam avon ashmah: 'lest they bring upon them guilt of iniquity (avon ashmah).' The combination of avon (iniquity, the internal state of wrongness) with ashmah (guilt, the objective liability incurred) creates a compound term for culpability. be'ochlam et-qodsheihem: 'by eating their holy things' — 'their holy things' refers to the Israelites' offerings that are now priestly property. The priest who allows a non-eligible person to eat these offerings brings guilt upon the entire system, since the holy offerings represent the nation's covenant relationship with YHWH.
17: vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: The formula opens a new legislative unit on standards for acceptable offerings (vv17-25). These transition formulae divide Leviticus into discrete legal pericopes; ch22 alone contains multiple such markers (vv1, 17, 26). Each new formula signals a fresh topic while remaining within the larger unit of priestly holiness. The structure implies that priestly law was delivered in discrete units, each with its own focus — no single speech act covers the whole.
19: lirzonchem tamim zachar: 'for your acceptance (lirzonchem) it shall be a male (zachar) without blemish (tamim).' Ratzon (acceptance, goodwill, delight) is the sacrificial goal — YHWH's favorable reception of the offering. Tamim (complete, without blemish, whole) from the root t-m-m suggests wholeness and integrity — the same word used of Noah ('blameless in his generation,' Gen 6:9) and Abraham ('walk before me and be blameless,' Gen 17:1), showing that the completeness required in sacrificial animals is the same moral standard for covenant people. Hebrews 9:14 applies tamim directly to Christ: he offered himself 'without blemish' to God.
23: shoru ve'seh saru'a veqaluah: 'An ox (shor) or a sheep (seh) that is overdeveloped (saru'a, lit. excessive/protruding) or stunted (qaluah, underdeveloped/contracted).' Saru'a (from sara', to overflow/exceed) refers to a limb disproportionately large; qaluah (from qalah, to be slight/diminished) refers to one that is shortened or withered. nedavah ta'aseh oto: 'you may offer it as a freewill offering (nedavah).' Animals with developmental anomalies cannot be used for vowed offerings (where the highest standards apply) but may be used for freewill offerings where the worshiper volunteers beyond obligation. velenidar lo yeratzeh: 'but for a vow it will not be accepted (lo yeratzeh).' The vow demands the best; freewill offerings have more flexibility.
26: vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: Another transition formula opens a sub-unit on the timing of offerings (vv26-30) and the holiness of YHWH's name (vv31-33). The law that follows (v27) sets a minimum age of seven days for sacrificial animals — paralleling the circumcision on the eighth day for human males (Gen 17:12): sacrificial eligibility begins after the first week of life is complete, once the animal is established. The transition formula marks this as a distinct divine speech, though closely related to the blemish laws above.
